The first one that's going to come up over and over again and comes up in all of cognitive neuroscience, cognitive psychology, whatever, is about representation, and in particular about mental representation.
There's information in the world, somehow it becomes internalized in our mind.
And the questions are about what kinds of information gets represented, how do we process it, what are the kinds of constraints on what we represent.
And we actually talked about this on Monday when we talked about that concept of the receptive field of that neuron.
We said that a neuron in the visual cortex, we might map that with the neurophysiological methods, to see that it represents a particular location in space.

We talked about a receptive field, we're talking about what's the representation representation, or what is that neuron, what is it capable of representing, or what does it contribute to in terms of representation.

So representations are really about the content of thought, but they also can be about how is that information, lots of the study's gonna be is how does that information, how do we internally represent information in the world.
So for instance, here's something here, think about what does that represent to you.
Okay, x squared plus y squared equals z squared, you're all saying, wait a second, I've heard that before.

Some of you probably remembered, oh yes, it represents a right triangle, it could represent a three-sided polygon with a right angle, it represents an equation, it represents the Pythagorean theorem, right?
This one equation here actually can represent, we might represent it in many different ways.
And of course we might see that across individuals, there's differences in how they represent the same sort of external stimulus.

But in addition to representations, really much of what cognitive psychology's about, and been taken up in cognitive neuroscience, is not just about the content, as I said, about what's being represented, but how do we manipulate that information.
And of course it's being manipulated when we go through the nervous system from light falling on the retina to somehow having an image in our brain, there's been many transformations.

We can describe those transformations in terms of the neural activity that underlies it, but cognitive psychologists at least are really interested in mentally what are those sorts of operations, what are those transformations that take the input and manipulate the information in certain ways, and those things are gonna reflect both our capabilities and the limitations of our ability to represent information.
So these two concepts are really central to cognitive psychology, to cognitive neuroscience, and cognitive therapy.

They're also always interested about asking, when a person's in a bad mental state, are they representing certain types of information in an abnormal or in an inappropriate way, and the therapy might be geared to trying to change the way they think about that thing.
The important thing is that we talk about how we manipulate information, but I wanna emphasize that how we represent information is gonna influence how we process that information.

All right. So then there's a, and when we turn now to cognitive neuroscience, okay, the kinds of experiments where people frequently test cognitive models or develop cognitive models is called neuropsychology.
That's the word you'll see in the literature frequently. And what does it really mean?
It means that I'm kind of doing psychological testing, but maybe using neurological populations to ask psychological questions.

Okay. So these typically, neuropsychology tends to refer to behavioral experiments that involve people who have some sort of neurological disorder or abnormality, or it might even be, you know, people with just sort of some sort of atypical nature, like it might be developmental conditions, like autism or something.
Just asking, can I characterize the difference in this population?
In cognitive neuroscience, there might be an interest about then relating that to changes in particular brain structures.
And so the question frequently then is about is, how is behavior disrupted when a particular part of the system or part of the brain is damaged?

And so I'm going to be doing these psychological experiments, and it might be like, say, comparing people with damage in a particular part of the brain with people who don't have it to ask about, you know, do these people actually miss more Fs than others or something like that?

So I'll give you an example.
